sections.title: How the system is structured and governed

blocks.html: Brazil runs the world’s highest-performing aluminum-can recovery system without a consumer deposit. The legal foundation is theNational Solid Waste Policy (PNRS, Law 12.305/2010)and its implementing decree, which require producers, importers, distributors, and retailers of disposable packaging to finance and operatelogística reversa(reverse-logistics) systems.

For aluminum cans, the sector signs a federalCommitment Agreementadministered byAbralatas(can manufacturers) andAbal(aluminum producers). Producers pay into a stewardship pool that guarantees purchase of 100% of the UBC scrap generated. Because aluminum scrap has a high commodity value, the system is largely self-financing: the sale of clean UBC covers collection and transport, with producer fees filling any gaps.

A distinctive governance feature is the formal integration ofcatadores— independent waste pickers and cooperatives — as recognized service providers. The PNRS explicitly includes them, and the Abralatas/Recicla Latas model channels revenue to cooperative collection points rather than relying solely on municipal contracts.

items: Decentralized collection:a nationwide web of cooperatives, voluntary drop-off points (PEVs), scrap merchants, and municipal programs. Only ~60% of Brazilian municipalities offer recycling collection, yet the can recovery rate is >95% because the informal/cooperative layer fills the gap.

Regional recycling hubs:the largest isPindamonhangaba, São Paulo, a historic can-sheet and recycling cluster with capacity of roughly 250 t/day. Novelis and Aleris Latasa operate major re-melters and can-sheet plants.
Speed:the average can is collected, recycled, and back on shelves in about30 days.
Automation:collection is mostly manual or semi-mechanized; sorting relies on eddy-current and manual separation at MRFs and cooperative facilities.

blocks.html: In 2024 Brazil recycled97.3%of aluminum beverage cans sold — the 15th consecutive year above 95%, amounting to33.9 billion cans (~417,700 t). The rate slipped from 100% in 2022–2023 mainly because a 64% surge in aluminum scrap exports pulled material away from domestic recyclers.

Economic returns flow through the high scrap value. A tonne of clean UBC is worth substantially more than an equivalent mass of mixed recyclables, so cooperatives prioritize cans. Over the past decade the program claims to have avoided18 Mt CO₂eand17 Mt of bauxite extraction. No transparent public cost-per-tonne for the can-specific collection stream was found; the system’s surplus comes from scrap resale rather than from public appropriations.

Brazil is a continental country with uneven municipal waste services, a large informal waste-picker workforce, and strong domestic can-sheet and re-melting capacity (Novelis, Aleris). The model works because:

items: the metal is valuable enough to pay for its own collection;
cooperatives provide low-cost, geographically distributed labor; and
domestic recyclers are hungry for feedstock, so scrap is purchased quickly.

blocks.html: The 2024 export surge is a warning: even a mature closed loop can leak material if foreign buyers pay more than domestic recyclers.

items: Producer stewardship + accessible drop-off beats deposit-only.A producer-funded system can reach 95%+ without a 10¢ consumer deposit if the economics are structured so that collection agents are paid immediately for clean UBC.

Formalize waste-pickers and community groups as collection partners.PR has active community organizations and informal can collectors; giving them a recognized role and a direct revenue share is faster than building a municipal fleet from scratch.
Protect domestic feedstock.Any PR law should require UBC to be offered first to the island plant before export, mirroring the EU’s new Waste Shipment Regulation “right of refusal” logic.
Build the re-melter before the rate peaks.Brazil’s loop is tight because re-melters and can-sheet plants exist. PR needs the secondary plant to exist so that collected cans have a buyer.

sections.title: How the system is structured and governed

blocks.html: Japan’s system is built on theContainers and Packaging Recycling Act (Law No. 112 of 1995, effective 1997). It is an Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) law, not a deposit-return scheme. Consumers sort and municipalities collect; beverage brand owners and packaging producers pay recycling fees to the government-designatedJapan Container and Package Recycling Association (JCPRA), which runs public tenders to select recyclers in each prefecture.

TheJapan Aluminum Can Recycling Association (JACRA)coordinates industry awareness and publishes the annual recovery and can-to-can rates. Fees are published per fiscal year by material category; for aluminum cans they have historically been around ¥70–100/kg, paid by producers into JCPRA. Municipalities receive support for collection and transport, while recyclers buy the sorted UBC from municipal yards.

items: Kerbside source separation is dominant.Households put aluminum cans into dedicated transparent bags; collection occurs on scheduled days separate from combustibles and PET. Public recycling stations sit in almost every neighborhood.
Vending-machine compressionis common in dense urban areas: many machines accept the empty can at the side of the unit, compacting it and improving logistics.
Domestic re-melters:Showa Denko (now Resonac), Daiki Aluminium, and Nippon Light Metal consume UBC and produce can-body sheet and other secondary alloys.

blocks.html: In FY2023 Japan collected97.5%of aluminum cans; in 2024 JACRA reported99.8%collection and75.7% can-to-can— meaning roughly three-quarters of recovered cans returned to new can production, with the remainder downcycled into other aluminum products (still in-country). Demand held steady at about2.09 billion cansin 2025 as population decline and PET substitution offset each other.

The fee-for-service model means producers bear the system cost, not consumers. This reduces “return friction” — residents do not need to visit a redemption center — but it requires a disciplined, high-compliance sorting culture and a capable municipal collection fleet.

Japan has high population density, strong local government capacity, a culture of household source separation, and domestic can-sheet and secondary smelting industries. The EPR model fits because municipalities can enforce sorting rules and producers are organized enough to collect and remit fees. It would be harder to replicate in a jurisdiction with weak municipal waste services and no domestic re-melter.

items: Separate “collection rate” from “closed-loop rate.”A 99% collection rate is impressive, but the economically meaningful number for a domestic plant is the share that stays on-island. PR should target both metrics explicitly.

EPR fees can substitute for deposits if collection discipline is high.If PR does not pass a 5¢ DRS, a producer-fee system modeled on JCPRA could still work — but only if municipalities deliver clean, sorted UBC to a central processor.
Transparent public fee schedules build trust.JCPRA’s annual per-material fee schedule is published; a PR PRO should do the same.
Convenience is the key driver.PR should place return points where people already shop — supermarkets, gas stations, pharmacies — rather than expecting dedicated trips.

sections.title: How the system is structured and governed

blocks.html: Europe is a patchwork moving toward harmonized high recovery. ThePackaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR, Regulation (EU) 2025/40), in force since February 2025, replaced the 1994 Directive. It mandates that member states hit80% separate collection of beverage cans by 2029 and 90% by 2033, and it effectively requires deposit-return schemes in most member states.
Two other laws shape aluminum specifically:

items: Critical Raw Materials Act (CRMA, 2024/1252):aluminum is listed as a strategic raw material; the EU aims for ≥25% of annual consumption to come from EU recycling by 2030, with stricter processing benchmarks for aluminum.

Waste Shipment Regulation recast (2024/1157):from 21 May 2026, member states must notify and justify exports of “green-listed” scrap, including non-hazardous aluminum UBC, giving domestic recyclers a right of refusal before material leaves the EU.

blocks.html: Governance is split: Brussels sets targets, member states design DRS and PRO structures, and industry associations (European Aluminium, Metal Packaging Europe) report aggregate rates and lobby for export curbs.

items: Mandatory DRSin 12+ member states by 2025 (Germany, Finland, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Estonia, Lithuania, Latvia, Ireland, Malta, Slovakia). Deposits are typically €0.10–0.25 per container, with dense Reverse Vending Machine (RVM) networks in every supermarket.
Kerbside and bring-bank systemspersist in non-DRS states (France, Italy, Spain), yielding lower and more variable recovery.

Can-sheet capacity:Germany’sNovelis NachterstedtandSpeira Voerdeplants, plus Novelis Göttingen and Pieve (Italy), Hydro (Norway/Germany), and Constellium (France), consume UBC for can-sheet production.

blocks.html: Europe reached a record76.3%aluminum beverage can recycling rate in 2023. Mature DRS states cluster at 90–99%; Germany leads at99%. New DRS launches have produced rapid step changes: Malta, Latvia, and Slovakia jumped from ~50% to >80% within 12–18 months of launch.

The economic driver is a combination of unredeemed-deposit (“breakage”) revenue and producer fees, which fund RVM networks and logistics. EU scrap prices in 2024 ranged roughly €1,400–1,800/t for clean UBC, linked to LME. The new export rules are expected to deepen the domestic closed loop by keeping material in-region.

Europe has high population density, integrated supply chains, and existing can-sheet capacity. The political consensus for circular-economy regulation is strong, and the single market makes cross-border scrap movements easy to monitor. The EU’s export curbs are a direct response to Asian demand pulling material out of the European loop.

items: Set binding recovery targets and ratchet them.The PPWR’s 80% by 2029 / 90% by 2033 framework gives investors certainty. PR could legislate 60% by 2030, 75% by 2033, and 85% by 2035.
Pair a DRS with a “right of refusal” for domestic industry.A PR law should require exporters to offer UBC to the island plant first, at market terms, before shipping scrap off-island.
DRS is the fastest lever.Europe’s new DRS states moved faster than its EPR-only states. A 5¢ DRS in PR is the most tested path to 75%+ recovery.

Recycled-content mandates create pull.The PPWR’s recycled-content targets give domestic recyclers a guaranteed buyer. PR could require that a rising share of cans sold on-island use post-consumer aluminum.

sections.title: How the system is structured and governed

blocks.html: The United States hasno federal packaging EPR or deposit law. Recycling is mostly funded by municipalities through general taxes, with revenue from commodity sales covering a declining share of costs. The result is a patchwork: ten states operate Container Deposit Laws (CDLs or “bottle bills”), while the rest rely on curbside single-stream, which recovers cans at much lower purity and rates.

The ten deposit states are California (CRV), Connecticut, Hawaii, Iowa, Maine, Massachusetts, Michigan, New York, Oregon, and Vermont. Deposits range from 5¢ to 10¢. Michigan and Oregon are at 10¢; California uses 5¢ for containers under 24 oz and 10¢ above. Industry groups — theAluminum AssociationandCan Manufacturers Institute (CMI)— publish an annual recycling-rate report and a roadmap targeting 70% by 2030, 80% by 2040, and 90% by 2050.

Packaging EPR is now advancing separately: Maine, Oregon, California, Colorado, Minnesota, Maryland, and Washington passed EPR/PRO laws in 2021–2024, with fees rolling out between 2027 and 2031. Aluminum cans will increasingly be covered by PRO fees, but deposit states still deliver the highest recovery.

items: Curbside single-stream dominatesin >70% of municipalities. MRFs use eddy-current separators and hand sorting, but contamination and breakage are high, and aluminum revenue is often used to subsidize lower-value materials.
Redemption centers and RVMsin deposit states capture the bulk of in-state UBC at higher purity and lower contamination.

Major domestic can-sheet capacity:Novelis (multiple plants), Arconic, JW Aluminum, and Constellium Muscle Shoals. The U.S. can remelt most of its UBC domestically, but a large share leaks abroad as scrap.

blocks.html: The U.S. aluminum can recycling rate fell to43% in 2023, down from a long-term average near 52% since 1990 — the lowest in decades. Of roughly100 billion cans placed on market(~1.7 Mt of aluminum), the recycled share was about730,000 tof UBC. More than61 billion canswere landfilled, representing nearly$1.2 billionworth of aluminum.

The divergence between deposit and non-deposit states is stark: deposit states consistently recover 60–90% of containers, while non-deposit states often fall below 30%. The Aluminum Association and CMI explicitly identify well-designed deposit-return systems as the most effective strategy to reverse the decline.

sections.title: How it fits the U.S. context

blocks.html: The U.S. has large, integrated aluminum and beverage industries, extensive MRF infrastructure, and strong domestic can-sheet capacity. The failure is policy, not technology: a federal system has been blocked by retail and beverage lobbying, leaving states to act alone. The result is that high-value aluminum is treated like a low-grade commodity in most of the country.

items: A state/territory-level DRS is the most-tested U.S. tool.Michigan’s 10¢ deposit and Oregon’s modernized 10¢ scheme show that deposits work when the value is high enough and redemption is convenient.

Single-stream alone is not enough.The U.S. decline proves that relying on curbside MRFs to recover cans fails at scale. PR should treat single-stream as a fallback, not the primary can-recovery channel.
Protect against export leakage.The U.S. exports ~30–40% of UBC and mixed aluminum scrap. PR should legislate a domestic-first offer before export, so the island plant receives the material.
Use PRO fees to backstop the DRS.Emerging U.S. EPR/PRO laws can fund education, RVM siting, and processing subsidies without replacing the deposit itself.

sections.title: How the system is structured and governed

blocks.html: Australia is thefirst continent fully covered by Container Deposit Schemes (CDS), after Tasmania launched Recycle Rewards on 1 May 2025. Each state and territory operates its own scheme; there is no federal harmonization. Deposits are uniformly10¢ per eligible container, funded by beverage producers and importers as a “first seller” fee, with consumers receiving the refund on return.

Key launch dates: South Australia (1977, pioneer), Northern Territory (2012), New South Wales (2017, “Return and Earn” run by TOMRA), ACT (2018), Queensland (2018, “Containers for Change”), Western Australia (2020), Victoria (2023, “CDS Vic”), and Tasmania (2025). Each scheme has a network operator or coordinator appointed by the state government.
The schemes are backed by national packaging policy under theRecycling and Waste Reduction Act 2020and the Australian Packaging Covenant, which sets mandatory targets and reporting.

items: RVM-dense networks:TOMRA is the dominant operator in NSW, QLD, and other states; Visy and Envirobank operate in Victoria. NSW alone hasmore than 600 return points(RVMs + depots).
Over-the-counter returnsat supermarkets, bottle shops, and community depots provide options for consumers who cannot use machines.

Collection logistics:returned containers are counted, compacted, baled, and sent to recyclers. Because the schemes divert the high-value fraction out of kerbside bins, MRFs lose some revenue — a known transition cost.

blocks.html: National aluminum can recovery climbed from ~67% before CDS rollout to roughly75–80%by 2024. Mature schemes such as South Australia and the Northern Territory exceed80%; NSW sits around70–75%. Victoria’s CDS Vic created an estimated597 direct jobsand reached >90% public awareness in its first eight months.

The economics rely onunredeemed deposits (“breakage”), which typically run 10–20% of containers and fund network operations. Producer fees net of breakage are roughly AUD$0.03–0.08 per can. Scheme operators report that a 10¢ deposit is high enough to motivate behavior but low enough to keep beverage prices stable.

Australia is a wealthy, urbanized, island continent with strong state governments and sparse rural populations. The state-by-state approach allowed early adopters (SA, NSW) to prove the model before laggards joined. However, Australia hasno major can-sheet rolling mill; most recovered UBC is exported to Korea, Thailand, India, or Indonesia for smelting. The loop is thereforebroken at the re-melting/rolling stage.

items: This is the most directly applicable model for an island.A 5¢ DRS with supermarket, mall, and civic drop-off points can lift recovery from <2% to 70%+ within a few years, just as Australia moved from 67% to 75–80%.
Keep the deposit lower than 10¢ to start.PR’s per-capita income and can volume argue for a 5¢ deposit initially; it can be raised after the network is dense.
Pair the DRS with a domestic re-melter.Australia shows that high recovery without local reprocessing still sends value offshore. PR’s plant is the missing second half of the Australian model.
Publish transparent tonnage and redemption data.Australian state schemes publish annual reports on return rates, jobs, and litter reduction, which builds political support.

sections.title: How the system is structured and governed

blocks.html: Iceland is an outlier: a small, cold-climate island that is simultaneously a global primary-aluminum powerhouse and a pioneer of national deposit-return.Endurvinnslan hf.was founded by law in 1989 (Law No. 52/1989, Regulation 368/2000) and is the only company authorized to pay deposits on bottles and cans. Iceland was one of the first countries in the world to implement a national DRS.

The system is governed by the Ministry of Environment and theIcelandic Recycling Fund (Úrvinnslusjóður), which executes extended producer responsibility for packaging, electronics, batteries, and vehicles. Beverage producers and importers pay fees into the fund, and the fund negotiates processing contracts with waste-management companies.
The deposit is currently a uniform16 ISK (~US$0.12)per container, with a 3 ISK handling fee. Unredeemed deposits are kept by Endurvinnslan to refinance the system.

items: 60 return facilities nationwide,including 10 automated locations where consumers do not need to sort or count containers manually.
Municipal recycling centersin every town accept aluminum cans in dedicated bins, often compacted on-site.
Geographic density:despite a sparse population, Iceland’s ~380,000 residents are concentrated in the Greater Reykjavík area and a handful of coastal towns, making a single national operator feasible.

blocks.html: Endurvinnslan reports a historical drink-container recycling rate of approximately85%, with aluminum cans near88%in recent years and around91%in 2021.Redemption is not the same as closed-loop can-to-can recycling:Iceland hasno domestic can-sheet or can-making capacity. The collected UBC is therefore baled and shipped abroad for re-melting and re-rolling; the metal may return to the island as new can sheet, but that second leg is produced in Europe or Asia, not Iceland. In other words, Iceland redeems cans very well, yet its beverage-can loop remains open at the rolling stage.

The primary-aluminum industry is large — three smelters produce roughly844,000 t/yrof P1020 ingot — but this is smelting, not can-sheet rolling, and it does not consume UBC. The distinction matters: a jurisdiction can be a world-class primary-aluminum producer and still not close the loop on its own used beverage cans.

Iceland’s geology and hydropower made it a primary-aluminum smelting hub (Rio Tinto Alcan at Straumsvík, Norðurál at Grundartangi, Alcoa at Reyðarfjörður). But smelting alumina into P1020 ingot is not the same as making can sheet. The island lacks the downstream rolling and can-making industry, so UBC must leave. Iceland’s lesson is thatprimary metal capacity does not automatically create a closed beverage-can loop; redemption rate is a collection metric, not a can-to-can remelt metric.

items: Scale is not a barrier if the network is dense enough.A jurisdiction of ~3.2 million people can support a national DRS and a single secondary plant far more easily than Iceland’s 380,000 support their system.

A single national operator simplifies governance.Endurvinnslan’s monopoly model reduces coordination costs. PR could appoint one territory-wide scheme coordinator (public or nonprofit) instead of 78 separate municipal programs.

Do not confuse primary smelting with recycling.A secondary UBC-to-ingot plant is a different, far less energy-intensive asset than a primary smelter. PR should build the secondary plant first; primary smelting is not required.
Keep the deposit simple and uniform.Iceland uses one deposit value for all materials, reducing consumer confusion. PR should start with one clear deposit for aluminum cans and add glass/PET later.
